text.title: Fitness Functions as Code Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules with rationale + source for fitness-functions-as-code. Includes terminal skip-this-methodology rule.

rule.statement: Each fitness function MUST live in its own test file (`tests/fitness/test_<axis>.py`); mixing axes in one file destroys diagnosability.

rationale.source: Ford et al, Building Evolutionary Architectures (2017)

rule.rationale: Ford et al, Building Evolutionary Architectures (2017) — atomic fitness functions are the smallest revertable unit.

rule.statement: Each test MUST be either binary (pass/fail on a hard rule) or windowed (compare metric to threshold ± tolerance); free-form assertions are rejected.
rule.rationale: Architecture sonar (ThoughtWorks 2019) — non-windowed assertions are fragile and produce flaky CI.

rule.statement: Modularity functions MUST be expressed as graph rules over the import / call graph (e.g. 'no module in `domain/` imports from `infra/`').
rule.rationale: Hexagonal / clean architecture — boundary violations only catchable as graph rules.

rule.statement: Latency / throughput functions MUST run against a deterministic load harness with pinned input + warm-up; flaky perf tests poison the gate.
rule.rationale: Brendan Gregg perf primer — flaky perf tests are the dominant cause of perf-gate abandonment.

rule.statement: Security characteristics (no high-CVSS findings, no secrets in repo) MUST be wired as fitness functions, not separate ad-hoc scans.
rule.rationale: Treating security as a separate process loses the merge-gate property; fitness-as-code unifies the gate.

rule.statement: Each fitness function MUST link the ADR it enforces (file path + line); orphan fitness functions get deleted on drift review.
rule.rationale: ADR-linkage is the rationale source-of-truth; without it, functions are arbitrary tests.

rule.statement: If the codebase is a single-file script or no ADRs / architectural characteristics are agreed, return `skipped`.
rule.rationale: Avoids invented fitness functions with no architectural target.
